subsystem.function: Knuckle-crossing wiring with service loops and flex-rated bend radius; connection types and load-path closure

instruction: Design only this subsystem: P0 plan, part_tree children, 12-24 coordinate-bearing geometry_ops. Every evidence_feature must appear. Do not modify other subsystems or the global envelope.

criteria.good: Battery, sensor, actuator, cartridge, payload, cable, or joint modules remain removable without full teardown.
criteria.bad: Functional modules are fused into a closed body.
criteria.loop_feedback: Separate the critical service module and add a visible removal path, latch, pin, or slide rail.

criteria.good: Harnesses, tubes, or service channels are routed through visible raceways or protected channels.
criteria.bad: Cables are buried inside closed links or shells with no inspection feature.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add external or inspectable harness channels with strain relief and access covers.

criteria.good: Access is represented by geometry: hatch, pocket, channel, fastener pattern, slot, window, or removable cover.
criteria.bad: Access is claimed only in prose.
criteria.loop_feedback: Convert service-access text into physical geometry_ops: cover, pocket, slot, window, and fastener pattern.

criteria.good: Every cable exit shows strain relief geometry (clamp, grommet, tie point) and routing respects a stated minimum bend radius away from moving joints and sharp edges.
criteria.bad: Cables exit bare holes, cross hinge lines unsupported, or bend around sharp printed edges with no relief feature.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add strain-relief clamps or grommets at every cable exit, state a minimum bend radius, and reroute harnesses away from hinge lines and sharp edges.

criteria.good: Connectors are reachable without disassembling structure, have finger/tool clearance, and mention keying or labeling to prevent mis-mating.
criteria.bad: Connectors face closed walls, sit in unreachable pockets, or identical adjacent connectors have no keying or label.
criteria.loop_feedback: Reorient connectors toward access openings, add finger clearance pockets, and engrave keying/ID labels next to each connector.

criteria.good: Per-finger linkage cartridges are removable by single pin or visible hinge access.
criteria.bad: Finger linkages are glued, buried, or represented only as a glove shell.
criteria.loop_feedback: Model per-finger cartridges with hinge pin holes, thimble sockets, and visible single-pin removal path.

criteria.good: Flexion-resistance actuator modules plug in and remain visible near knuckle row.
criteria.bad: Actuators are bonded or hidden without connector/service geometry.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add plug-in actuator pockets, connector openings, knuckle-row mounts, and service labels.

criteria.good: Rigid mock assembly preserves soft-contact cues, wrist quick release, and ergonomic clearance.
criteria.bad: Rigid shell ignores don/doff, comfort, and finger clearance.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add wrist quick release, soft-contact interface zones, finger clearance, and cable raceway inspection.

criteria.good: Finger and wrist wiring uses service loops, strain relief at every flex boundary, and a stated bend radius sized for repeated flexion cycles.
criteria.bad: Wires run taut across knuckles or terminate at rigid mounts with no relief, inviting fatigue failure.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add service loops and strain relief at each knuckle and wrist crossing, and state a flex-rated bend radius.

criteria.title: Electronics, Signal, and Motor Wiring Judgment

criteria.good: Motor power lines and sensor/encoder signal lines run in separate channels or are spaced/crossed at right angles, with the separation visible in routing geometry.
criteria.bad: Power and signal wires share one tight bundle along the whole route with no separation or shielding note.
criteria.loop_feedback: Split power and signal into separate raceways or add spacing/shielding notes where they must share a channel.

criteria.good: Each electronics module states its grounding scheme (star point, chassis bond, isolated) and signal returns route alongside their signals.
criteria.bad: Grounding is unmentioned; returns are implied through structure with no bond feature or note.
criteria.loop_feedback: Add a named ground/bond feature per module and state how each signal's return path is routed.

criteria.good: PCBs mount on standoffs with keep-out clearance to walls and moving parts, connector edges face access openings, and mounting holes match standard board patterns.
criteria.bad: Boards are floated or glued in cavities with no standoffs, clearance, or connector orientation logic.
criteria.loop_feedback: Mount each board on standoffs with stated keep-out clearance and orient connector edges toward access openings.

criteria.good: Every inter-part interface names a specific connection type (bolted, threaded_insert, snap_fit, press_fit, dovetail_slide, clevis_pin, revolute_bearing, band_clamp, adhesive_lap, o_ring, connector_mate, harness_passthrough) with its DOF and a numeric clearance.
criteria.bad: Interfaces are labeled only 'mount' or 'attach' with no connection type, DOF, or clearance.
criteria.loop_feedback: For each interface pick a connection type from the taxonomy, state its DOF (0/1R/1T/1R1T/3R), and add a numeric clearance and the matching geometry feature.

criteria.good: Parts can be installed in a stated order where every fastener, connector, and mating feature stays reachable; no installed part blocks access to a later one; last-installed and captive parts are identified.
criteria.bad: Assembly order is unstated or impossible — a part traps another, or a fastener ends up behind a wall with no access.
criteria.loop_feedback: State an installation order and verify each joint is reachable when it is made; reorder or add access openings where a part would trap another.

criteria.good: One primary datum part anchors the assembly; each other part locates off a named parent datum using a 3-2-1 scheme, so tolerance does not accumulate uncontrolled across the stack.
criteria.bad: Parts float with no shared datum, or each part references a different feature so stack-up grows part-to-part.
criteria.loop_feedback: Name one primary datum part, route each part's location back to it through one parent datum, and keep critical fits off the primary datum to limit stack-up.

criteria.good: Every part's primary load traces through named interfaces to the base/ground; the load path closes for all parts and high-load joints use load-appropriate connection types.
criteria.bad: One or more parts carry load with no continuous path to ground, or a high-load joint uses a low-load connection (e.g. snap-fit holding a structural member).
criteria.loop_feedback: Trace each part's main load to the base through named interfaces; upgrade any load-bearing joint from snap/adhesive to bolted/pinned/clamped and add the fitting where the path breaks.

criteria.good: Compared to the previous version the design moves the right direction: fewer interfaces, parts consolidated where service is preserved, open load paths closed, and redundancy added only at critical joints.
criteria.bad: Iteration adds interfaces or loose parts without justification, or consolidates in a way that buries service access.
criteria.loop_feedback: Drive the next version toward fewer justified interfaces, consolidated printable parts that keep service access, and closed load paths; add redundancy only at safety-critical joints.

criteria.good: The assembly uses the fewest interfaces that still allow service and manufacture; each remaining interface is justified by service, motion, manufacture split, or load transfer.
criteria.bad: Many redundant or unexplained interfaces, or arbitrary part splits that add joints without a service/manufacture reason.
criteria.loop_feedback: Merge interfaces that exist for no service/motion/manufacture/load reason; keep a split only when it earns its joint.
